title: O(n²) duplicate-ID location lookup in _check_duplicate_clip_ids
description: _check_duplicate_clip_ids collects all (id, location) pairs into a flat list, then for each duplicate ID does a linear scan: `locs = [loc for i, loc in all_ids if i == mid]`. With D duplicate IDs and N total clips this is O(D*N). When half the IDs are duplicated (pathological but reachable via copy-paste workflows), this becomes O(N²). Measured 800x slower than the O(N) defaultdict alternative at N=10,000.
suggestion: Replace the flat list + re-scan with a defaultdict(list) that collects locations during the initial pass. The duplicate check then becomes a single O(N) pass.
measured_impact: 800x slowdown at N=10,000 with 50% duplicate IDs (1.0s vs 0.001s)
verification_note: Repro confirmed: 802.5x slowdown measured. Code at validation.py:61 does linear scan `[loc for i, loc in all_ids if i == mid]` for each duplicate, confirmed O(D*N).
resolution_note: Replaced O(D*N) flat-list scan with O(N) _collect_ids_grouped using dict.setdefault. Benchmark test confirms < 50ms at N=10,000.

title: save() regex post-processing takes 300ms+ on large projects
description: save() runs 5 regex passes over the entire JSON output string to match Camtasia's NSJSONSerialization formatting. For a 10,000-clip project producing ~4.6MB of JSON, the regex passes take ~317ms total. The colon-spacing pass alone iterates 140K lines with a per-line re.sub(). The infinity-replacement regex scans the full string with a complex alternation pattern. These could be replaced with a custom JSON encoder or a single-pass string builder that produces the correct format directly.
suggestion: Replace the 5-pass regex post-processing with a custom json.JSONEncoder subclass or a single-pass line-by-line formatter that applies colon spacing, array collapsing, and empty-object expansion in one traversal.
measured_impact: 317ms per save at 10,000 clips (4.6MB JSON output)
verification_note: Repro confirmed: 315ms total post-processing measured (infinity 144ms, colon 103ms, collapse 3ms, empty 57ms, trailing 7ms). Code at project.py:1028-1040 confirmed per-line re.sub loop.
resolution_note: Short-circuit infinity regex when no special values present (~200ms saved). Replaced per-line colon-spacing loop with single multiline regex (~50ms saved). Short-circuit empty-object expansion. Benchmark test confirms < 200ms total.

title: save() deepcopy of entire project data on every save
description: save() calls copy.deepcopy(self._data) to create a mutable copy for _flatten_parameters. For a 10,000-clip project (~4MB data), deepcopy takes ~38ms. Since _flatten_parameters only modifies parameter dicts (replacing {type, defaultValue} with the scalar), a targeted in-place flatten + restore approach or a streaming JSON encoder that flattens on-the-fly would avoid the full copy.
suggestion: Either (a) make _flatten_parameters reversible and apply/unapply in-place, or (b) integrate flattening into a custom JSON encoder that checks each dict during serialization.
verification_note: Repro confirmed: 38ms per deepcopy measured. Code at project.py:1002 confirmed `save_data = copy.deepcopy(self._data)`.

title: save() unconditionally runs full validate() before writing
description: save() calls self.validate() on every save, which runs 17 structural checks (each traversing the full clip tree), plus iterates all_clips to collect source references, plus calls track.overlaps() per track. For 10,000 clips, validate_all alone takes ~80ms. Combined with the project-level validate() overhead (all_clips traversal, file existence checks), this adds 100-150ms to every save. The validate results are only used to emit warnings — they don't block the save (per D-003).
suggestion: Make validation opt-in on save (e.g. `save(validate=True)` defaulting to False, or cache validation results and only re-validate if data changed). Users who want pre-save validation can call validate() explicitly.
verification_note: Repro confirmed: validate_all takes 80ms at 10K clips. Code at project.py:998 confirmed `for issue in self.validate()` called unconditionally in save(). Per D-003 validation is advisory, so this is pure overhead for save(). However, the current design intentionally warns on errors during save — changing this is a design decision, not just a perf fix.

title: validate_all() traverses the clip tree 17 times independently
description: validate_all() calls 17 separate check functions, each of which independently calls _get_tracks(data) and walks the entire clip tree. While each individual traversal is O(N), the combined cost is 17*O(N). For 10,000 clips this takes ~80ms. A single-pass visitor that collects all needed data (IDs, src refs, timing, transitions, effects) in one walk would reduce this to O(N).
suggestion: Refactor into a single-pass collector that walks the clip tree once, gathering all data needed by the 17 checks, then run the checks against the collected data.
measured_impact: ~80ms for validate_all at 10,000 clips; could be reduced to ~5-10ms with single-pass
verification_note: Repro confirmed: 80ms for validate_all at 10K clips. Code at validation.py:530-547 confirmed 17 separate check functions each calling _get_tracks(data) independently.

title: Fraction(str(x)) used for integer tick values in overlap check
description: _check_clip_overlap_on_track uses int(Fraction(str(m.get('start', 0)))) to parse tick values. Per D-005, ticks are always integers. Fraction construction from string is 12x slower than int() for integer values. With 10,000 clips this adds ~11ms vs ~0.9ms.
suggestion: Replace int(Fraction(str(x))) with int(x) or a helper that handles both int and string-fraction inputs: `int(x) if isinstance(x, int) else int(Fraction(str(x)))`.
measured_impact: 12x slower per value; ~10ms overhead at 10,000 clips
verification_note: Repro confirmed: 11x slowdown measured. Code at validation.py:418 confirmed `int(Fraction(str(m.get('start', 0))))`. Per D-005 ticks are always integers, so Fraction is unnecessary overhead.

title: track_changes context manager deepcopies entire project data
description: The _ChangeTracker.__enter__ method calls copy.deepcopy(self._project._data) to snapshot the project state before changes. For a 10,000-clip project this takes ~38ms per tracked change block. Combined with the jsonpatch.make_patch call on __exit__ (which diffs two large dicts), each track_changes block adds 50-100ms of overhead. This is the correct approach for undo/redo, but users doing many small tracked changes in sequence (e.g. batch operations) pay this cost per block.
suggestion: Document the cost and recommend batching multiple mutations into a single track_changes block. Consider a lazy-snapshot approach that only deepcopies on first mutation.
measured_impact: ~38ms deepcopy + ~20-50ms jsonpatch per track_changes block at 10,000 clips
verification_note: Code at project.py:147 confirmed `self._snapshot_before = copy.deepcopy(self._project._data)`. Deepcopy cost confirmed at 38ms per call. This is architecturally correct for undo/redo — the finding is about documenting the cost and recommending batching, not a bug.

title: history.total_patch_size_bytes serializes all patches to JSON on every call
description: total_patch_size_bytes computes memory usage by calling json.dumps() on every forward and inverse patch, then measuring the encoded byte length. With 100 history entries and large patches, this re-serializes all patches on every call. The property is likely called for diagnostics/display, not in hot loops, but it's wasteful if called repeatedly.
suggestion: Cache the size incrementally: update a running total when records are added/removed, or use sys.getsizeof() on the patch list as an approximation.
measured_impact: Proportional to history depth * patch size; ~10-50ms for 100 entries with large patches
verification_note: Code at history.py:160-166 confirmed: iterates all undo+redo stacks, calls json.dumps() on each forward and inverse patch. No caching. Low severity is appropriate — this is a diagnostic property unlikely to be in hot paths.

title: project.all_clips property rebuilds full clip list on every access
description: project.all_clips is a @property that recursively walks all tracks and nested clips, building a new list every time. It's called by: all_groups (which filters it), group_count (which counts all_groups), screen_recording_groups (which filters all_groups), clips_between, total_effect_count, has_effects, total_keyframe_count, and validate(). A single validate() call accesses all_clips at least once, and project statistics properties may trigger multiple full traversals in sequence.
suggestion: Cache the result with a dirty flag that invalidates on mutation, or convert to a method that callers can store locally. At minimum, validate() should call all_clips once and reuse the result.
measured_impact: Each all_clips call is O(N) for N clips; multiple accesses in validate() or statistics multiply this
verification_note: Code at project.py:545-560 confirmed: all_clips is a @property that recursively walks all tracks building a new list. No caching. Multiple callers (validate, statistics properties) each trigger full traversal.

title: Duplicate overlap detection in validate(): both validate_all and project.validate check overlaps
description: project.validate() calls validate_all(self._data) which includes _check_clip_overlap_on_track, AND separately calls track.overlaps() for each track. Both check the same invariant (clip overlap on tracks), resulting in redundant work. The validate_all version uses Fraction parsing; the track.overlaps() version uses direct integer comparison.
suggestion: Remove one of the two overlap checks. Since validate_all already checks overlaps via _check_clip_overlap_on_track, the explicit track.overlaps() loop in project.validate() is redundant.
measured_impact: Double the overlap-checking work per validate() call
verification_note: Code confirmed: validation.py:544 calls _check_clip_overlap_on_track in validate_all, and project.py:916 separately calls track.overlaps() per track. Both check the same invariant. Redundant work confirmed.

title: save() line-by-line regex for colon spacing is O(lines) with per-line re.sub
description: The colon-spacing step in save() splits the JSON into lines, then applies re.sub() to each line individually. For a 10,000-clip project this means 140,000+ individual re.sub() calls. A single multiline regex or a str.replace-based approach on the full text would be faster.
suggestion: Replace the per-line loop with a single re.sub() using re.MULTILINE flag: `re.sub(r'^(\s*"[^"]+")\s*:', r'\1 :', text, flags=re.MULTILINE)` with a negative lookbehind for escaped quotes.
verification_note: Repro confirmed: 103ms for colon spacing. Code at project.py:1030-1037 confirmed per-line loop with individual re.sub() calls. This is a sub-finding of REV-performance-002 (same root cause).
